title: The Scars He Kept
summary: When Jesus rose from the dead, He kept His scars. He showed them to Thomas — not as a rebuke, but as proof that love always leaves a mark.
suggestedHymns: I Know That My Redeemer Lives
bodyText:
When Jesus rose from the dead, He could have had any kind of body. He could have returned without a trace of what happened on Friday. Glorified. Unblemished. Untouched.

But He didn't.

He kept the scars.

When Thomas doubted, Jesus didn't argue with him. He didn't lecture him about faith. He simply said:

"Put your finger here; see My hands. Reach out your hand and put it into My side. Stop doubting and believe." — John 20:27

And Thomas fell to his knees: "My Lord and my God!"

Why did Jesus keep the scars?

Because scars are proof. Not of weakness — of love. They are evidence that something costly happened. That a wound was endured. That a price was paid.

The scars in His hands tell us that the nails were real. The wound in His side tells us that the spear was real. And the resurrection tells us that His sacrifice was accepted.

There is a beautiful thought in this: in all of eternity, the only man-made things in heaven will be the scars on the body of Jesus.

No monuments. No trophies. No buildings. Just the marks of a love that bled.

And those scars are not a source of shame for Him. They are a source of identity. Revelation describes Him in heaven as "a Lamb, looking as though it had been slain." Even in glory, He bears the evidence of the cross.

So when you take this bread today, remember — you are not remembering a theory. You are remembering a body that was pierced and still bears the proof.

When you take this cup, remember — you are not participating in a tradition. You are remembering blood that was real, shed by a Savior who chose to keep the scars so we would never forget.

Thomas saw them and believed. We remember them and give thanks.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Night He Was Betrayed
summary: Paul anchors the Lord's Supper to a specific night — the night Jesus was betrayed. The timing matters. Jesus gave us this meal in His darkest hour.
suggestedHymns: When I Survey the Wondrous Cross
bodyText:
Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 11:23:

"The Lord Jesus, on the night He was betrayed, took bread…"

That phrase — "on the night He was betrayed" — is not just a timestamp. It is a window into the heart of Christ.

Think about what was happening that night. Judas had already made his deal. The religious leaders were finalizing their plot. Peter was about to deny Him. Every disciple would scatter before sunrise.

Jesus knew all of this. He knew who would betray Him. He knew who would deny Him. He knew what the next twelve hours would bring — the garden, the arrest, the trial, the scourging, the cross.

And in that moment — not in a moment of triumph, not after the resurrection, not from a place of comfort — Jesus took bread, gave thanks, and broke it.

He gave us this meal from the valley, not the mountaintop.

That tells us something important: the Lord's Supper was not born out of celebration. It was born out of sacrifice. It was given to us by a Savior who was hours away from the worst suffering in human history — and He spent that time thinking about us.

"This is My body, given for you. Do this in remembrance of Me."

He was about to be broken. And yet His concern was that we would remember.

"This cup is the new covenant in My blood. Do this, whenever you drink it, in remembrance of Me."

Paul adds in verse 26: "For whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until He comes."

Every time we gather at this table, we are announcing to the world — and reminding ourselves — that on the worst night of His life, Jesus gave us the greatest gift of His love.

So take this bread with reverence. Take this cup with gratitude. And remember: He gave us this meal not when it was easy, but when it cost Him everything.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Passover Fulfilled
summary: The Passover lamb pointed forward for centuries. When Jesus went to the cross, the shadow became substance. Our Passover Lamb has been sacrificed.
suggestedHymns: Are You Washed in the Blood
bodyText:
On the night Israel was delivered from Egypt, God gave them specific instructions. Take a lamb — unblemished, without defect. Kill it at twilight. Take the blood and put it on the doorposts of your house.

And God said in Exodus 12:13:

"When I see the blood, I will pass over you. No destructive plague will touch you when I strike Egypt."

The blood of the lamb stood between God's people and judgment. Not their goodness. Not their effort. The blood.

For over a thousand years, Israel repeated that meal. Every Passover, they retold the story. They remembered the lamb. They remembered the blood. They remembered that God had delivered them.

And then, one Passover night in an upper room in Jerusalem, Jesus took the bread and the cup and changed everything.

He was the Lamb the Passover had been pointing to all along.

Paul says it plainly in 1 Corinthians 5:7:

"For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed."

Every lamb slaughtered in Egypt. Every lamb offered on the temple altar. Every drop of blood on every doorpost for a thousand years — all of it was a shadow. The substance was Christ.

His blood does not just cover a doorway. It covers a life.
His sacrifice does not just delay judgment. It removes it.

So when we take this bread, we remember a body that the Passover anticipated for centuries.
When we take this cup, we remember the blood that God has always required — and that Christ alone could provide.

The lamb has been slain. The blood has been applied. And death passes over.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: A Table in the Wilderness
summary: David said God prepared a table for him in the presence of his enemies. The Lord's Supper is that table — set for us even in the middle of a broken world.
bodyText:
David writes in Psalm 23:5:

"You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies."

Notice where the table is set. Not in a palace. Not behind locked doors. Not after the enemies have been defeated.

In the presence of enemies. In the middle of the fight. In the wilderness, where danger is real and the ground is uncertain.

And yet — there is a table.

God does not always remove the difficulty before He provides the comfort. Sometimes He prepares the table right in the middle of it.

That is what the Lord's Supper is.

We live in a world that is broken. Relationships strain. Bodies fail. Temptation presses in. Loss is real. And yet every Lord's day, God sets a table for us.

Not a table of escape — but a table of presence. A reminder that He is with us, even here. Even now.

Paul calls it "the cup of blessing" and "a participation in the body of Christ" (1 Corinthians 10:16). It is communion — not just with bread and juice, but with the living God who feeds us in the wilderness.

Maybe you came this morning carrying something heavy. A worry. A grief. A struggle you haven't told anyone about.

God knows. And He has prepared a table for you — not after the battle, but in the middle of it.

This bread — His body, sustaining you.
This cup — His blood, covering you.
This table — His presence, surrounding you.

Even in the wilderness, He provides.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Darkness at Noon
summary: For three hours, darkness covered the land while Jesus hung on the cross. That darkness was not weather — it was the weight of sin being laid on the sinless Son of God.
suggestedHymns: Alas, and Did My Savior Bleed
bodyText:
Mark 15:33 records something that has no natural explanation:

"At noon, darkness came over the whole land until three in the afternoon."

Three hours of darkness. In the middle of the day. The sun did not simply dim — it withdrew. As if creation itself could not bear to watch what was happening on that cross.

And from the center of that darkness came the most agonizing cry in all of Scripture:

"My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?" — Mark 15:34

Those words were not metaphor. They were not dramatic effect. Jesus, the eternal Son of God, who had never for one moment been separated from His Father — experienced separation.

Why?

Because our sin was placed on Him.

Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 5:21: "God made Him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God."

That is what the darkness was about. It was the moment when the sinless One absorbed the full weight of a world's rebellion. The Father did not look away out of cruelty — He turned His face because holy justice required it. Sin and holiness cannot coexist. And in that moment, Jesus bore it all.

So that we would never have to.

The darkness was temporary. Three hours. And then it lifted. Because the price had been paid. The separation was endured. And the way to the Father was opened again — not for Jesus, who had always been there, but for us, who had never been able to reach Him on our own.

This bread — a body that endured the darkness for us.
This cup — blood shed in those terrible hours so we could walk in light.

The darkness has passed. The Son has risen. And we are no longer forsaken.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Bought at a Price
summary: Paul tells the Corinthians twice: you were bought at a price. The Lord's Supper is a weekly reminder that we belong to the One who purchased us.
suggestedHymns: Take My Life and Let It Be
bodyText:
Paul says it twice in 1 Corinthians — once in chapter 6, and again in chapter 7 — because apparently we need to hear it more than once:

"You are not your own; you were bought at a price." — 1 Corinthians 6:19–20
"You were bought at a price; do not become slaves of human beings." — 1 Corinthians 7:23

Bought at a price. That is marketplace language. Redemption language. It means someone paid something to acquire us.

In the ancient world, slaves could be purchased and set free. The price was paid. The chains were removed. The person walked away belonging to a new master — or to no one at all.

But Paul says we were bought — and now we belong to God. We are not our own.

That should change how we think about everything. Our bodies, our time, our choices, our ambitions — none of it is ultimately ours. It belongs to the One who paid for it.

And what was the price? Not silver. Not gold. Peter tells us that. The price was the precious blood of Christ.

So this table is a reminder of ownership. Not oppressive ownership — the kind that crushes. But loving ownership — the kind that frees.

We were slaves to sin. He bought us out. We were captive. He paid the ransom. And now we belong to Him — not because we earned it, but because He purchased it.

This bread — the body that was the price.
This cup — the blood that sealed the transaction.

You have been bought. You are valued. And you belong to a God whose purchase of you is the greatest proof of your worth.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Until He Comes
summary: Every Lord's Supper is both a look back and a look ahead. We remember His death — and we proclaim it until He returns.
suggestedHymns: When We All Get to Heaven
bodyText:
Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 11:26:

"For whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until He comes."

Two words at the end of that sentence carry enormous weight: until He comes.

This means the Lord's Supper is not only a memorial. It is a proclamation with an expiration date. We will not do this forever. There is a day coming when the bread and the cup will no longer be needed — because we will see Him face to face.

Every Lord's day, we look back. We remember the cross. We reflect on the body broken and the blood shed.

But we also look forward. We declare, by this very act, that this is not the end of the story. He is coming back.

The early church lived with that anticipation. They gathered on the first day of the week, broke bread together, and said to each other — and to the watching world — "He died. He rose. And He's coming again."

Revelation 22:20 captures that longing:

"He who testifies to these things says, 'Yes, I am coming soon.' Amen. Come, Lord Jesus."

So this table faces two directions. Behind us — the cross. Ahead of us — the crown.

This bread points back to a body given. But it also points ahead to a day when every tear will be wiped away.
This cup points back to blood shed. But it also points ahead to a feast in the presence of the King.

We are not just remembering. We are waiting. And one day, the waiting will be over.

Until then, we proclaim His death. Until He comes.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Stone the Builders Rejected
summary: The world rejected Jesus. But God made Him the cornerstone of everything. At this table, we honor the One the world threw away.
suggestedHymns: Christ Is Made the Sure Foundation
bodyText:
Peter writes in 1 Peter 2:4:

"As you come to Him, the living Stone — rejected by humans but chosen by God and precious to Him…"

Rejected by humans. Chosen by God. Precious.

That is the paradox of the cross. The One the world threw away is the One God exalted. The stone the builders looked at and said, "We don't need this" — God made the cornerstone of everything.

Psalm 118:22 prophesied it long before it happened: "The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone."

And that is exactly what happened at Calvary. The religious leaders rejected Him. The Roman government discarded Him. The crowd chose a criminal instead. They looked at Jesus and saw someone expendable.

But God saw something else. He saw the foundation of salvation. The cornerstone of a new covenant. The living Stone on which the church would be built.

Peter continues in verse 7: "Now to you who believe, this stone is precious."

Precious. That is the word. The same One the world counted as worthless, we count as priceless.

And that is what this table is about. At this table, we do the opposite of what the world did at the cross. The world rejected Him — we receive Him. The world mocked Him — we worship Him. The world threw Him away — we hold Him close.

This bread represents the body the world discarded — but we treasure.
This cup represents the blood the world spilled — but we honor.

The builders may have rejected Him. But He is our cornerstone. And everything we are rests on Him.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: What Wondrous Love
summary: Paul builds a case for the love of God in three layers — while we were powerless, while we were sinners, while we were enemies. Each layer makes the love more astonishing.
suggestedHymns: O How He Loves You and Me
bodyText:
In Romans 5, Paul builds a case for the love of God — and each layer makes it more staggering.

First, verse 6:

"You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly."

Powerless. Unable to save ourselves. Unable to fix what was broken. And that is when Christ died.

Then verse 7:

"Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person someone might possibly dare to die."

Paul acknowledges reality. Human love has limits. We might — might — sacrifice for someone noble. Someone deserving. Someone who has earned it.

But then comes verse 8:

"But God demonstrates His own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us."

Not while we were worthy. Not while we were improving. Not while we were reaching out to Him.

While we were sinners.

That is the defining difference of God's love. Human love responds to goodness. God's love responds to need. Human love rewards the deserving. God's love rescues the undeserving.

And the proof is not a promise written in the sky. The proof is a cross planted in the ground. A real body. Real blood. A real death — for people who were still in rebellion against Him.

That is what this table celebrates. Not that we loved God — but that He loved us first. Not that we came to Him — but that He came for us.

This bread — a body given for the powerless.
This cup — blood shed for the ungodly.

What wondrous love is this.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: He Became Poor
summary: Jesus did not just leave heaven — He emptied Himself of glory so that we, in our spiritual poverty, could become rich.
bodyText:
Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 8:9:

"For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though He was rich, yet for your sake He became poor, so that you through His poverty might become rich."

Think about what Jesus left behind.

He was rich. Not in earthly terms — but in eternal ones. He possessed the full glory of heaven. The worship of angels. Unbroken communion with the Father. Authority over everything that exists.

And He walked away from all of it.

He became poor. Born in a borrowed stable. Raised in an obscure village. "Foxes have dens and birds have nests," He said, "but the Son of Man has no place to lay His head."

He was mocked by the people He created. Betrayed by a friend He had chosen. Stripped naked and executed between criminals on a garbage heap outside Jerusalem.

That is poverty. Not just material poverty — but the poverty of rejection, abandonment, and bearing a world's worth of sin.

And why? "So that you through His poverty might become rich."

Not rich in money. Rich in grace. Rich in forgiveness. Rich in hope. Rich in access to God. Rich in an inheritance that can never perish, spoil, or fade.

That is the exchange of the cross. He took our poverty — spiritual bankruptcy, hopelessness, death — and gave us His riches — life, peace, and eternity with God.

This bread — the body of the One who became poor for us.
This cup — the blood that purchased our eternal wealth.

He gave up everything so that we could have everything that matters.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.
